summary: Jeff Atwood argues that as CPU development shifts from faster clock speeds to multiple cores, software developers face a serious challenge: our programming languages and mental models are fundamentally ill-suited for concurrent programming. He draws on predictions from 2001 about multi-core processors and quotes several experts who warn that threading is extremely difficult to get right. Using Dan Appleman's vivid analogy comparing multithreading to a psychokinetic explosive, Atwood emphasizes that incorrect threading designs can be catastrophically expensive to fix. He concludes that without radical changes to programming paradigms, developers will struggle to adapt to a concurrent world.
key_points: CPU development is shifting from faster clock speeds to multiple cores, forcing developers to write concurrent code to gain performance
Current programming languages provide inadequate support for concurrent programming, making it unnecessarily difficult
Threading bugs are uniquely dangerous because design flaws cannot be patched later and have unlimited potential debugging costs
Most programmers lack experience with concurrent programming and underestimate its complexity
Linear thinking dominates programming, but concurrency requires reasoning about events that can interrupt each other at any time
money_quotes: We're pretty screwed if the only way to get more performance out of our apps is to make them heavily multithreaded.
We'll have to have much better language-level support for concurrent programming. What we have now sucks. Rocks. Hard.
Multithreading is the software equivalent of a nuclear device because if it is used incorrectly, it can blow up in your face.
Used incorrectly, it can destroy your reputation and your business because it has nearly infinite potential to increase your testing and debugging costs.
If your design is incorrect, it will be virtually impossible to patch up the problems later. And again, the potential cost to fix threading problems has no upper limit.
When you can't go forward, go sideways: more CPUs on the same die.
Writing software is hard enough as-is without adding a pinch of 'the most complex code in our app' throughout... your app.
key_insight: The shift to multi-core processors represents a fundamental challenge for software development because our languages, tools, and ways of thinking are built for linear execution, not concurrency.
